title: Process Control of Variables and Materials
narrationScript: Every call you make as a Level Two — true, false, relevant, rejectable — rests on one assumption: the process that produced the indication was in control. Penetrant testing fails silently. An overwashed part, a tired penetrant, a caked developer all produce hardware that simply looks clean. So the method is governed twice. First, control the variables: a clean, dry surface; part and penetrant between forty and one twenty-five degrees Fahrenheit; at least ten minutes of wet dwell; rinse water at fifty to one hundred degrees and no more than forty p s i; a dryer that never takes the part past one sixty; a thin developer film given at least ten minutes; and a thousand microwatts per square centimeter of UV-A over a booth darker than two foot-candles. Second, test the system on the rhythm ASTM E1417 sets: the known-defect panel and contamination checks daily, remover concentration and ambient light weekly, water content monthly, brightness quarterly. When any check fails, the line stops — and every part since the last passing check is suspect.

sections.heading: An Indication You Can Trust
sections.body:
The last three lessons taught you to classify indications — true or false, relevant or nonrelevant — and to evaluate what remains against an acceptance standard. All of it rests on one unstated assumption: **the process that produced the indication was in control.** Penetrant testing fails silently. An overwashed line, a penetrant tank slowly diluting with rinse water, a dryer running hot, a caked developer — none of them announce themselves. They hand you parts that look clean, and at the inspection station a clean-looking part and a flawless part are indistinguishable.

**ASTM E1417** answers that with a closed system of control: materials qualified to **AMS 2644** and listed on its Qualified Products List; a written procedure that fixes every variable inside the limits the practice allows; and a schedule of in-use tests — **E1417 Table 1** — that verifies the materials and the integrated system at fixed intervals.

The Level II responsibility is blunt. Before you interpret a single indication, you should be able to show that the day's checks were performed, passed, and documented. If they were not, you are not evaluating parts — you are guessing.

sections.heading: Front of the Line: Surface, Temperature, Dwell
sections.body:
Process control starts before penetrant ever touches metal.

- **Surface condition.** The part must be visually clean and **dry** at penetrant application. After aqueous cleaning, an unbroken water film — no water breaks — signals freedom from oily soils; water or solvent left sitting in a flaw dilutes penetrant exactly where it matters most.
- **Temperature.** E1417 holds part and penetrant between **40 and 125 °F (4 and 52 °C)**. At the cold end, viscosity climbs and capillary filling slows — procedures extend dwell. Above the band, volatile constituents flash off and the film dries instead of penetrating. Outside the band you need a procedure qualified for that temperature, not improvisation.
- **Dwell.** Minimum **10 minutes**, and the film must stay **wet** the entire time — a dried film stops feeding the flaws, and the recovery is cleaning and full reprocessing. Tight fatigue cracks and cold parts earn extended dwell per the written procedure.
- **Coverage.** The area of interest plus a margin, wetted completely. A dry streak is an uninspected part that will still be stamped as inspected — unless the process controls catch it.

sections.heading: Back of the Line: Removal, Drying, Development, Light
sections.body:
Removal is where most process failures live, and each method carries its own controls.

- **Method A — water-washable:** coarse spray at **50–100 °F (10–38 °C)**, maximum **40 psi (275 kPa)**, washed under UV-A on fluorescent work so you can watch the background leave and stop the moment it has. Overwash strips the flaws silently.
- **Method B — lipophilic:** applied by immersion or flowing — never brushing — with no agitation during contact, and contact time held to the procedure, never more than **3 minutes**, then rinsed immediately.
- **Method C — solvent:** a clean, dry, lint-free wipe first, then a *lightly* solvent-dampened wipe. Flushing the surface with solvent is prohibited.
- **Method D — hydrophilic:** a plain-water prerinse to knock down bulk penetrant, then remover at controlled concentration — up to **35 %** for immersion baths, **5 % or less** for spray — verified weekly by refractometer, then a final rinse.

Downstream, the dryer holds part temperature at or below **160 °F (71 °C)** for the minimum effective time — excess heat bakes penetrant out of the very flaws you charged. Developer goes on as a thin, even film and gets at least **10 minutes** of development before reading. At the booth: **UV-A of 1,000 µW/cm² minimum**, verified daily at **15 in (38 cm)** from the filter face and after every bulb change; ambient white light at or below **2 fc (21.5 lx)**; visible-dye work needs **100 fc (1,076 lx)** minimum at the surface. Give your eyes at least **1 minute** of dark adaptation, and leave photochromic lenses in your locker.

sections.caption: Every station on the penetrant line carries a controlled variable with a hard limit — and the daily known-defect panel rides the entire chain before any production part does.

sections.heading: System Performance: Prove the Whole Chain Daily
sections.body:
Each material test isolates one ingredient. None of them proves the **system** — penetrant, remover, dryer, developer, lamp, and operator working together. That is the job of the **system performance check**: a known-defect standard processed through the complete line, exactly as a production part would be, **each day the line runs, before production hardware**.

The common standard is the cracked-chrome **PSM-type panel** (such as TAM 146040): a stainless panel carrying **five graded crack centers**, largest to smallest, in a chrome-plated band, plus a **grit-blasted strip** that evaluates washability and residual background. Process it, read it under UV-A, and compare against the panel's **baseline record** — what it showed when the system was known to be healthy.

- **Pass:** every baseline crack center shows at comparable brightness, and the grit-blasted strip washes to its normal background. Release the line.
- **Fail:** any center missing or dim, or background high. Stop, find the variable that moved, correct it, and re-verify — and every part processed since the last passing check is suspect.

Treat the panel like an instrument. Clean it scrupulously after every use — penetrant left in the cracks today is a false *pass* tomorrow. Keep it dedicated to one process line, and retire it when the crack centers degrade.

sections.caption: A PSM-type panel carries five graded crack centers and a grit-blasted washability strip. Run it through the complete process and compare it to baseline before any production part is processed.
Key in-use system checks — intervals per ASTM E1417 Table 1; your written procedure governs the working targets.
rows: Known-defect panel through the complete process
All baseline indications at comparable brightness; background normal
Visual check of the tank under white light and UV-A
No water layer, scum, dirt, or color/odor change
Fluff check; 4 in. (100 mm) circle of powder viewed under UV-A
Fluffy, not caked; fewer than 10 fluorescent specks
Wetting/coverage and fluorescent contamination; concentration by hydrometer or refractometer
Uniform wetting, no fluorescent background; concentration per procedure
Refractometer reading against the bath's target
Radiometer at 15 in. (38 cm) from the filter face
Ambient white light in the darkened booth
Calibrated light meter at the inspection surface
Water content — water-washable penetrant and lipophilic emulsifier
Water-content test on a tank sample
ASTM E1135 comparison against a sealed unused reference sample
Calibrated temperature check of the oven
Within procedure tolerance; parts never above 160 °F (71 °C)

sections.heading: Testing and Maintaining the Materials Themselves
sections.body:
In-use materials drift. Open tanks collect rinse water and shop dust; dyes fade with heat and UV exposure; remover concentration creeps as water evaporates. The Table 1 rhythm exists to catch the drift before parts pay for it.

- **Penetrant.** Daily: look for a water layer, scum, sediment, or a change in color or smell. Monthly, for water-washable penetrant in open tanks: water content, with **5 % as the ceiling** — rinse water dripping back in slowly turns the penetrant into its own remover. Quarterly: fluorescent brightness per **ASTM E1135** against a **sealed reference sample retained from new stock** — in-use penetrant must deliver at least **90 %** of the reference brightness. Dye damage cannot be stirred away; a failed tank is replaced.
- **Removers.** Hydrophilic bath concentration weekly by refractometer — too rich overwashes the flaws, too lean leaves fluorescent background everywhere. Lipophilic emulsifier: water content monthly, same 5 % limit.
- **Developers.** Dry powder daily: it must stay **fluffy** — caking means moisture — and a 4 in. circle under UV-A must show **fewer than 10 fluorescent specks**, or the powder is hauling penetrant from part to part and seeding false indications. Aqueous baths: daily for wetting and fluorescent contamination, weekly for concentration.

Three maintenance rules carry the rest. **Adjust only what is adjustable** — concentration can be corrected and retested; contamination and dead dye cannot. **Never intermix** — a tank is topped off only with the identical qualified product, never another manufacturer's, type, method, or sensitivity level. **Cover the tanks** — lids between shifts keep out the water and dust the daily checks keep finding.

sections.title: A failed check stops more than the line
sections.body: Every process-control test bounds your exposure. When one fails, three things happen: the line **stops**; the cause is found, corrected, and **re-verified**; and **every part processed since the last passing check is reexamined**. That is why the panel runs daily, before production — so a bad day can never silently become a bad week. Document the failure, the fix, and the disposition of the exposed parts. A check that fails quietly into a logbook nobody reads is worse than no check at all.

figures.title: Process control points on the penetrant line
The daily system performance check: PSM-type panel

interactive.title: The E1417 Testing Rhythm
interactive.intro: ASTM E1417 Table 1 puts every in-use check on a fixed interval. Sort each check into the interval it carries.

items.label: Known-defect panel through the complete process
items.why: System performance is verified daily, before production parts run — it is the only check that proves the whole chain works together, not just one ingredient.

items.label: In-use penetrant checked for water, scum, or color change
items.why: Penetrant contamination is a daily visual check under white light and UV-A — open tanks collect rinse water and shop soil constantly.

items.label: Dry developer checked for caking and fluorescent specks
items.why: Daily: the powder must stay fluffy, and a 4-inch circle under UV-A must show fewer than 10 fluorescent specks — contaminated powder seeds false indications on clean parts.

items.label: UV-A lamp intensity measured at 15 inches
items.why: Daily, and again after every bulb change. The floor is 1,000 µW/cm² at 15 in (38 cm) from the filter face.

items.label: Hydrophilic remover concentration by refractometer
items.why: Weekly. Concentration creep is slow but deadly — too rich overwashes flaws, too lean leaves fluorescent background.

items.label: Aqueous developer concentration by hydrometer
items.why: Concentration is a weekly check — the bath loses water to evaporation and drag-out. Contamination, by contrast, is checked daily.

items.label: Water content of open-tank water-washable penetrant
items.why: Monthly, with 5 % as the ceiling — rinse water dripping back into the tank slowly turns the penetrant into its own remover.

items.label: Fluorescent brightness against the sealed reference sample
items.why: Quarterly, per ASTM E1135 — in-use penetrant must deliver at least 90 % of the unused reference's brightness, or the tank is replaced.

checkQuestions.question: Penetrant brightness, remover concentration, and developer condition all passed their individual tests. Why does ASTM E1417 still require a known-defect panel to be processed daily before production?
options: Because the panel is the only legal record that an inspector was present that day
Because individual material tests expire after 24 hours under E1417
Because each material test isolates one variable — only a panel processed through the complete line proves the integrated system can still find flaws
Because the panel is used to recalibrate the UV-A radiometer before use
checkQuestions.explanation: Each Table 1 material test examines a single ingredient in isolation. The system performance check is the end-to-end proof: penetrant, removal, drying, developer, lighting, and technique all have to work together to reveal the panel's known crack centers at baseline brightness.

checkQuestions.question: How is the fluorescent brightness of in-use penetrant verified, and what is the acceptance limit?
options: Quarterly, by comparison against a sealed sample of unused penetrant per ASTM E1135 — at least 90 % of the reference brightness
Daily, with a radiometer reading of at least 1,000 µW/cm² at 15 inches
Weekly, with a refractometer reading within the procedure's tolerance
Monthly, by confirming water content stays at or below 5 %
checkQuestions.explanation: Brightness is the quarterly check, performed per ASTM E1135 against a retained, unused reference sample; below 90 % the dye has degraded and the tank is replaced. The radiometer checks the lamp, the refractometer checks hydrophilic remover concentration, and the 5 % limit belongs to the monthly water-content test.

checkQuestions.question: Tuesday's morning panel run fails — the two smallest crack centers do not show. Monday's run passed and Monday was a full production day. After the cause is corrected and re-verified, what happens to Monday's production parts?
options: They ship — Monday's panel passed before they were processed
Only the parts that showed recorded indications are reviewed
They are rejected outright and scrapped
They are reexamined — every part processed since the last passing check is suspect, because the system could have drifted at any point after Monday's panel
checkQuestions.explanation: A passing check vouches for the system only at the moment it ran. The exposure window opens at the last passing check, so Monday's parts are suspect — not automatic scrap — and must be reexamined through the corrected, re-verified system.

checkQuestions.question: Which in-use check is performed weekly with a refractometer?
options: Dry developer fluorescent contamination
Hydrophilic remover bath concentration
Lipophilic emulsifier water content
Penetrant brightness
checkQuestions.explanation: Hydrophilic remover baths are checked weekly with a refractometer against the procedure's concentration target. Dry developer contamination is a daily UV-A check, lipophilic emulsifier water content is monthly, and brightness is the quarterly E1135 comparison.

checkQuestions.question: During the daily dry-developer check, a 4-inch circle of powder examined under UV-A shows about a dozen fluorescent specks. The powder is still fluffy. What is the correct response?
options: Continue using it — fluffiness is the only daily requirement
Oven-dry the powder to drive off the fluorescence and reuse it
Replace the developer — at 10 or more fluorescent specks it is contaminated with penetrant and can transfer false indications to parts
Increase development time to compensate for the contamination
checkQuestions.explanation: Fluorescent specks are penetrant carried back from processed parts. At 10 or more in the 4-inch sample the powder is rejected: contaminated developer deposits glowing specks on clean hardware, manufacturing false indications. Heat does not remove dye, and the fluff check only screens for moisture caking.

references: CP-105-2024 PT Topical Outline §3.6.7 (process control: variables, testing and maintenance of materials)
ASTM E1417/E1417M, Standard Practice for Liquid Penetrant Testing (process limits; Table 1 in-use tests and intervals)
SAE AMS 2644, Inspection Material, Penetrant (material qualification and Qualified Products List)
ASTM E1135, Standard Test Method for Comparing the Brightness of Fluorescent Penetrants (quarterly brightness test method)
